nativeRange.habitat: Rocky and coral reefs at depths of approximately 4–75 m; typically aggregates near the reef bottom in small schools. Range extends from the Gulf of California south to Banderas Bay (Cabo San Lucas area), with recent records pushing northward.

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding primarily on zooplankton (planktivore tendency) and has also been observed consuming fish eggs. In captivity, generally accepts a varied diet consisting of meaty items such as frozen mysis shrimp and brine shrimp, herbivore flake foods, and frozen preparations. Vitamin-enriched foods are commonly recommended to help preserve coloration. Two to three small feedings per day tend to reduce competition stress in group-housed specimens.

summary: The Limbaughi Chromis is a schooling damselfish endemic to the Gulf of California and eastern Pacific coast of Mexico. Juveniles are bright blue with a yellow tail fin; adults deepen to blue-purple. The species tends to be much milder-mannered than most damselfishes and is generally considered reef-safe.
careNotes:
The Limbaughi Chromis is generally advised to be kept in small groups of at least three. Like other chromis species, solitary individuals can become subdued, and groups tend to develop dominance hierarchies among themselves. The species is best suited to a reef system with ample rockwork and open mid-water swimming space that reflects its natural rocky reef habitat.

The juvenile color phase is notably distinct from the adult: young fish are bright blue with a yellow caudal fin, and the yellow fades as the fish matures, with the body deepening to blue-purple. Juveniles are sometimes sold under different names in the trade as a result.

Despite a semi-aggressive temperament rating sometimes applied to the species, the Limbaughi Chromis is widely described as atypically mild-mannered for a damselfish and considerably less aggressive than similar-looking species within the family. The semi-aggressive label largely reflects within-group hierarchy behavior rather than aggression toward unrelated tankmates.

The species is gonochoristic (meaning each fish maintains a fixed sex and does not change sex as it matures, unlike some reef fish). Mating in the wild is polygamous. All available specimens are typically wild-caught, as the species has not been reported successfully bred in captivity.

Because this species is best kept in groups and benefits from open swimming space, a minimum of 200 L is advisable to accommodate a small shoal.
breedingNotes: In the wild, males prepare nesting sites and guard demersal eggs — eggs that sink and adhere to a substrate — aerating them until they hatch. The species has not been successfully bred in captivity, and all available specimens are wild-caught. Captive breeding should be considered extremely unlikely with current methods.

fish.breedingNotes: Has not been successfully bred in captivity; all trade specimens are wild-caught. In the wild, males prepare nests and guard demersal eggs.
